messages.text: You aren't the only one looking for telemetry management: https://github.com/jkingsman/Remote-Terminal-for-MeshCore/issues/32 . RemoteTerm does support fetching telem on repeaters, including battery and LPP sensors, via the web interface. Its API makes querying them easy, but I'd prefer to keep that as a "script uses our API" pattern rather than building that in to try to stay away from any kind of autonomous message sending that isn't linked to a clear trigger (e.g. message outgoing/incoming/bots). The example script I added should do what you're looking to do and output to a CSV; setting up a scheduled task to do that under linux with crontab should be super simple. (edited)

messages.author: usulsuspct
messages.text: It looks like if a path is known then 3x tries before reverting back to flood. From the docs: 5.3. Q: What happens when a node learns a route via a mobile repeater, and that repeater is gone? A: If you used to reach a node through a repeater and the repeater is no longer reachable, the client will send the message using the existing (but now broken) known path, the message will fail after 3 retries, and the app will reset the path and send the message as flood on the last retry by default. This can be turned off in settings. If the destination is reachable directly or through another repeater, the new path will be used going forward. Or you can set the path manually if you know a specific repeater to use to reach that destination. In the case if users are moving around frequently, and the paths are breaking, they just see the phone client retries and revert to flood to attempt to re-establish a path.
messages.content_html: It looks like if a path is known then 3x tries before reverting back to flood. From the docs: 5.3. Q: What happens when a node learns a route via a mobile repeater, and that repeater is gone? A: If you used to reach a node through a repeater and the repeater is no longer reachable, the client will send the message using the existing (but now broken) known path, the message will fail after 3 retries, and the app will reset the path and send the message as flood on the last retry by default. This can be turned off in settings. If the destination is reachable directly or through another repeater, the new path will be used going forward. Or you can set the path manually if you know a specific repeater to use to reach that destination. In the case if users are moving around frequently, and the paths are breaking, they just see the phone client retries and revert to flood to attempt to re-establish a path.

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: I am asking this for all companion applications. could you make sending messages in multi-byte option per channel and per contact? I imagine for a while even after all repeaters are updated to 1.14+, there will be channels you want to send with 1-byte to get 64 hops, and then there will be channels you want to send with 2- or 3-byte to disembogue repeater IDs and fewer hops. Another example: as a regional mesh updating their repeaters to 1.14+, I may want to create a #multibytepublic hashtag channel to test multibyte with others in public. We can do this to test 1.14 repeater firmware take rate and coverage. We would need to send only multibyte messages in #multibyte_public, but keep regular public channel in 1-byte path hash.

messages.text: Ahh, yes. --force-recreate won't actually rebuild (if you're using build: . in your docker compose) nor pull a new image (if you're using image: docker.io/jkingsman/remoteterm-meshcore:latest ). To do a full rebuild (the former), run docker compose up --force-recreate --build . If you're instead using remoteterm-meshcore:latest as your image specifier, run docker compose pull before your docker compose up --force-recreate . You can always validate your running version at /#settings/about
